does: This paper identifies feature starvation (dead neurons) in sparse autoencoders as a fundamental geometric instability rather than merely an empirical problem. The authors show that the ℓ1-induced sparse coding map is mathematically unstable when paired with shallow amortized encoders in overcomplete dictionaries. They propose Adaptive Elastic Net SAEs (AEN-SAEs), a fully differentiable architecture that combines an ℓ2 structural regularization term to enforce strong convexity and Lipschitz stability with adaptive ℓ1 reweighting to eliminate shrinkage bias and suppress spurious features. This approach eliminates the need for expensive heuristic resampling and nondifferentiable hard-masking methods while improving the recovery of monosemantic features that represent interpretable concepts in language models.
connects: This work extends the long lineage of sparse coding and dictionary learning research, connecting classical sparse regression theory to modern interpretability challenges in large language models. It builds on recent work using sparse autoencoders to disentangle polysemantic representations in LLMs, responding to practical problems like feature starvation that plague standard ℓ1-regularized approaches. The paper incorporates insights from elastic net regularization, a classical statistical method for balancing bias-variance tradeoffs in high-dimensional settings. It also engages with optimization theory literature on the stability properties of sparse recovery algorithms and the geometric properties of overcomplete dictionaries, grounding practical neural network design in rigorous mathematical foundations developed in signal processing and statistics.
enables: By establishing a theoretically-grounded solution to feature starvation, this work enables more reliable and efficient interpretability methods for very large language models. The fully differentiable nature of AEN-SAEs removes computational bottlenecks that previously required expensive post-hoc corrections, making dictionary learning more practical at scale. This creates opportunities for better unsupervised discovery of interpretable features in transformer internals, potentially supporting mechanistic interpretability research and safety applications. The geometric perspective on sparse coding instability may inform future designs of other autoencoder variants and could extend to other domains requiring interpretable learned representations. Improved feature recovery could accelerate research into understanding and controlling internal representations in increasingly capable language models.
